title: Estrada v. Desierto — Estrada v. Arroyo: Constitutional Succession to the Presidency
primary_topics: Estrada v. Desierto / Estrada v. Arroyo
sub_topics: April 3, 2001 Motion for Reconsideration unanimously denied
Edgardo Angara Diary as 'authoritative window on state of mind'
EDSA II vs EDSA I (1986) distinction
Lawyers League for a Better Philippines v. Aquino
Clinton v. Jones (US Supreme Court immunity precedent)
House Resolutions 175, 176, 178 confirming Arroyo and Guingona
Cardinal Sin pastoral letter demanding resignation
Clarissa Ocampo / Edgardo Espiritu impeachment testimony
Mercado, Reyes, Lacson withdrawal of support
Inhibition rationale: 'hold myself above petitioner's reproach'

stances.claim: Arroyo is the de jure President because Estrada had resigned before her noon January 20, 2001 oath-taking — proven by 'totality test' using prior, contemporaneous, and posterior acts; his press release was a 'valedictory, his final act of farewell. His presidency is now in the past tense.'
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal-evidentiary totality framing + finality aphorism (Puno ponencia)

stances.claim: Estrada's January 20 letter to the Senate and House Speaker claiming 'temporary leave of absence due to inability to govern' commands scant legal significance — 'petitioner's resignation from the presidency cannot be the subject of a changing caprice nor of a whimsical will especially if the resignation is the result of his repudiation by the people.'

Arroyo is not 'merely acting President' — she took her oath as President (not acting), the House Resolutions 175/176 supported her assumption as President, the Senate confirmed her VP nominee Guingona, both houses started sending bills to her, and the Senate declared the Impeachment Court 'functus officio.'

EDSA II is distinguishable from EDSA I — EDSA I's Aquino government 'was the result of a successful revolution by the sovereign people' under an abrogated 1973 Constitution with the new Freedom Constitution decreed (per Lawyers League v. Aquino, beyond judicial scrutiny); EDSA II's Arroyo government remains under the 1987 Constitution with all institutional structures intact.

(Vitug concurring) The ascension of Arroyo has resulted in neither rupture nor abrogation of the legal order — 'an insistence that the events in January 2001 transgressed the letter of the Constitution is to ignore the basic tenet of constitutionalism and to fictionalize the clearly preponderant facts.'

(Mendoza concurring) The 1941-1943 Quezon-Laurel two-presidents situation 'is turning somersault with history' — the Philippines had two presidents then because there were two governments (Laurel's Japanese-belligerent-occupant de facto vs Quezon's de jure Commonwealth in exile); today there is only one government under the 1987 Constitution and therefore only one President.

(Bellosillo concurring) Permanent disability 'cannot realistically be given a restrictive and impractical interpretation as referring only to physical or mental incapacity, but must likewise cover other forms of incapacities of a permanent nature, e.g., functional disability' — objectively manifested by civil-society refusal, mass cabinet resignations, AFP/PNP withdrawal, congressional recognition, and international acceptance of Arroyo.

(Re: claim of resignation under duress) 'No tanks rumbled through the Palace, no attack planes flew over the presidential residence, no shooting, no large scale violence, except verbal violence' — pressure was exerted but did not vitiate voluntariness; Estrada's entourage even detoured to San Juan to bid goodbye to followers.
stances.rhetorical_move: structured-negation framing of duress claim + factual-contrast device

stances.claim: CJ Panganiban's voluntary inhibition (per Rule 137 second paragraph) 'did not arise from any legal ground showing partiality or bias' — he voluntarily resorted to nonparticipation 'to hold myself above petitioner's reproach' and to deprive 'him or anyone else [of] any excuse to cast doubt on the integrity of these proceedings,' applying pro hac vice only.

register_markers: extensive verbatim quotation of ponencia, concurrences, and separate opinions
Tagalog quotation (Pagod na pagod na ako)
Latin (functus officio, pro hac vice, de jure, de facto)
first-person inhibition exposition (CJ's voice inserted at end)
decision_framework_signals: applies 'totality test' (prior/contemporaneous/posterior facts) for ascertaining state of mind
uses functional-disability expansion to accommodate institutional-collapse incapacitation (Bellosillo)
frames EDSA II (constitutional succession under intact 1987 Constitution) vs EDSA I (revolution under abrogated 1973 Constitution) as a doctrinal distinction
uses Quezon-Laurel two-government historical-international-law framing to refute dual-presidency claim
applies Angara-Diary admissibility through admissions-against-interest / adoptive-admission / alter-ego doctrines
uses voluntary inhibition (Rule 137 second paragraph) as 'sound judicial discretion' framework distinct from mandatory disqualification
applies pro hac vice limitation to inhibition (case-specific, not future-blanket)
cross_references: See pp. 9-66 of this book (Chapter 2 — Old Doctrines and New Paradigms)
notable_anecdotes: Estrada's entourage detouring to the Municipal Hall of San Juan to bid goodbye to followers before going to Polk Street, Greenhills residence
The 11-10 senator-judges vote denying the prosecutor's motion to open the 2nd envelope (alleged P3.3B 'Jose Velarde' deposit)
Estrada's quote 'Pagod na pagod na ako. Ayoko na — masyado nang masakit' (I'm very tired. I don't want this anymore — it hurts too much)
Davide's January 22, 2001 unanimous Court Resolution confirming the oath-taking; Davide and Panganiban inhibiting from the merits in Estrada v. Desierto

Veterans Federation Party (party-list) precedent: the Court rejecting CJ's prior inhibition offer because his PCCI involvement was 'merely a voluntary non-compensated officer' and the case antecedents predated his PCCI tenure
